read: A compact-final TileLang dKV variant now works: it keeps the query-major MFMA contribution body and in-CTA compact group reduction, then atomic-adds reduced compact rows directly into final [groups, D] dKV instead of writing a compact partial-row stream. S128 correctness passes against a final-dKV PyTorch oracle. At S4096, however, the best final-atomic variant is 11.72 ms, slightly slower than the retained compact-partial producer at 11.60 ms. This means the current TileLang candidate is no longer dominated by final partial-row HBM output alone; contribution/body plus local accumulation are the active wall. The final-atomic path remains useful for memory and integration shape because it writes a 2 MiB final dKV instead of a 128-256 MiB partial workspace.

correctness_gate.shape: group_panel_size=128 capacity=96 topk_workspace=768 token_block=32 block_k=32 block_d=64 threads=256

comparison.read: Reduced final atomics remove the partial workspace but do not improve latency. Keep the compact-partial producer as the latency retained TileLang candidate; keep final-atomic as an integration/memory branch.

next_kernel_work: Fuse LI target_probs and grad_logits production into the retained TileLang producer so it stops reading materialized [S,K,H] diagnostic tensors.

Reduce contribution/body time rather than only output traffic: fewer padded selected slots, better MFMA tile legality than K32 for capacity96, or HIP/rocWMMA/CK control for segmented reduction without TileLang padding.
If integrating before the latency target is reached, use final-atomic mode to avoid the compact partial workspace, but do not count it as a speed win.
